## 08.12.2025

**Attendees:** María, Kursat, Nika, Anniina, Sarah

**Agenda**

- **Update on the** **scRNAseq analysis by Kursat (40 min)**

- **Wrap up + next meeting scheduling (5 min)**

### **Notes**

- **IDS vs PDS Focus:**

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Prioritizing IDS first and later validating in PDS may provide
 > clearer insights.

- However, for now Anniina suggests continuing with the analyses using
 > all samples. A clear description of the cohort is needed tho.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- **Correlation analysis:**

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Expect an anticorrelation between the "stroma-like" and "TSI-like"
 > signatures; the correlation plot will help assess whether
 > assigning cells based on the TSI-like score being greater than the
 > stroma-like score is appropriate. If two distinct populations are
 > visible in the plot (a), this assignment strategy is valid. If no
 > clear quadrant separation emerges, set a threshold by selecting
 > the top 30% highest-correlating features within each quadrant (b).

> ![](media/image1.png){width="1.2864588801399826in"
> height="1.2864588801399826in"}![](media/image3.png){width="1.4169772528433946in"
> height="1.2916666666666667in"}

a\. b.

## 

- **Fibroblast composition per patient:**

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Once the fibroblast-type annotations are refined, plot the

6in"
> height="1.2916666666666667in"}

a\. b.

## 

- **Fibroblast composition per patient:**

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Once the fibroblast-type annotations are refined, plot the
 > proportion of each fibroblast type per patient, and annotate this
 > by IDS/PDS to see if one or the other types are enriched in a
 > given condition.

> ![](media/image2.png){width="1.859375546806649in"
> height="1.3224431321084864in"}

- **Pathway analysis:**

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Results for the TSI-like fibroblasts are consistent with Nika's
 > findings from the GeoMX data. However, Nika will share the list of
 > pathways she used so that Kursat can rerun his analysis using the
 > same pathway set.

- For the stroma-like fibroblasts, several factors might explain the
 > current pathway patterns:

> **1)** **Sample dissociation effects:** incomplete digestion could
> reduce the recovery of stroma-like fibroblasts. Anniina believes this
> is unlikely, given the harsh dissociation protocol used in Turku.
>
> **2)** **Imbalanced cell numbers:** there is a strong discrepancy
> between TSI-like (\~17,379 cells) and stroma-like (\~529 cells).
> Downsampling may help mitigate this.
>
> **3)** **Number of most variable genes:** increasing the number of
> variable genes may improve performance; Kursat will rerun the analysis
> using 200 variable genes.

this.
>
> **3)** **Number of most variable genes:** increasing the number of
> variable genes may improve performance; Kursat will rerun the analysis
> using 200 variable genes.

- With the current analysis we don't have information about the
 > directionality (up/down-regulation). A next step will be to do
 > GSEA to examine directionality and FDR-corrected significance.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- **Additional GeoMX analyses:**

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Since the scRNA-seq analysis will include both PDS and IDS samples,
 > it will be useful to revisit the GeoMX data to evaluate whether
 > the TSI-like and stroma-like fibroblast signatures identified by
 > Nika remain consistent when all samples are combined.

- Additionally, given that the project is moving toward ADC-focused
 > work (functional experiments testing anti-TROP-2 ADCs in
 > combination with immunotherapy and no chemotherapy) it will also
 > be important to assess TROP-2 expression in the GeoMX dataset.
 > This analysis will be performed across all samples (PDS and IDS).

#### **Action items**

- María will create a slack channel and pin a link to the meeting
 > notes there

- Kursat will: 1) generate correlation plots and adjust thresholding
 > for fibroblasts annotations as needed based on that; 2) Plot
 > proportion of each fibroblast type per patient and annotate based

rate correlation plots and adjust thresholding
 > for fibroblasts annotations as needed based on that; 2) Plot
 > proportion of each fibroblast type per patient and annotate based
 > on PDS/IDS as well; 3) Refine the pathway analysis

- Nika will: 1) Share the set of pathways used in her analysis with
 > Kursat; 2) check whether signatures remain consistent when
 > including pre-treatment samples in the analysis; and 3) check
 > TROP-2 expression and generate plots.

- Follow up meeting on 13th of January (11:00 - 12:00).

## 23.10.2025

**Attendees:** María, Kursat, Sara, Nika, Anniina

**Agenda**

- **Intro from Nika to give context on the input given to Kursat (5
 > min)**

- **Update on scRNAseq analysis by Kursat (40 min)**

- **Next steps (10 min)**

- **Wrap up + next meeting scheduling (5 min)**

### **Notes**

1. Nika raised the point that we never had a meeting all together to
 > decide which direction we are going with this project.

2. Nika gave a short intro on what she has done so far. In the last
 > part we met, she presented that fibroblasts in the TSI expressed
 > genes that are related to ECM remodelling and immune signaling.
 > She also did the GSEA and found that the pathways are related to
 > metastasis and ECM remodelling. Very distinct phenotypes of CAFs
 > in the TSI compared to CAFs in stromal regions.

3.

o did the GSEA and found that the pathways are related to
 > metastasis and ECM remodelling. Very distinct phenotypes of CAFs
 > in the TSI compared to CAFs in stromal regions.

3. Nika gave Kursat the most variable genes that were defining each of
 > the spatial phenotypes.

4. It might be interesting to see if those specific CAFs are already
 > pre-existant in pre-chemo samples (there were paired samples in
 > IM's paper)

5. So Kursat have the top

6. Nika will give extra input to kursat

7. Kursat will re-run the script

8. María will create a dedicated slack channel to discuss prelim
 > results from any analysis

## 06.10.2025

**Attendees:** María, Kursat, Sarah

**Agenda**

- **Sample processing, e.g. who will do it? How to share samples for
 > each one\'s project?**

- **Project overlap/separation (María/Kursat)**

- **How to integrate ADCs?**

- **ADCs; limitations (For María: Number of ADCs/conditions,
 > Workflow/Time/Helping-hands regarding scRNA experiment)**

- **Mini update on scRNA analysis**

### **Notes**

9. Change the experimental design to include TWO ADCs, for that **we
 > will exclude chemo and talabostat from the original experimental
 > design**

> There are two options:
>
> 1\) include two anti-TROP2 antibodies with different payloads or
>
> 2\) include one anti-TROP2 and one anti-FRa
>
> There are risks associated to both of these options:

ons:
>
> 1\) include two anti-TROP2 antibodies with different payloads or
>
> 2\) include one anti-TROP2 and one anti-FRa
>
> There are risks associated to both of these options:

1\) Maybe we don't see a significant difference between the two
conditions.

> 2\) FRa-targeted ADCs have only proven effective in selected patients
> with very high FRa expression. Since our pilot is a cohort of only 4
> patients, the risk that only one is FRa high is quite high, and
> therefore we would be testing the therapy mostly in patients where it
> wouldn't be effective
>
> If we don't see a lot of differences between the two anti-TROP ADCs,
> the results would still be publishable, as we can report that they
> still work and that the effects on the TME cells are x and y. On the
> other hand, we could also test anti-FRa and see if the combination
> with ICI is synergistic, which is also interesting and publishable.
> However, this seems more risky. Also, the clinical relevance would be
> higher if we test anti-TROP2 because anyways the anti-FRa is only
> approved and most likely reimbursed to patients that are selected
> through the FRa biomarker (very high expression), so our results most
> likely wouldn't change this. **We will then include both of the
> anti-TROP2 ADCs, and keep the anti-FRa as backup**

10. We can still do flow cytometry paired with the scRNA-seq and look at

dn't change this. **We will then include both of the
> anti-TROP2 ADCs, and keep the anti-FRa as backup**

10. We can still do flow cytometry paired with the scRNA-seq and look at
 > the immune cell activation upon treatment as we are also testing
 > the possible synergy of ADCs in combination with immunotherapy.

11. **We need to determine the concentrations of the ADCs we want to
 > use. To do this, we will perform titration experiments.** Our
 > primary endpoint is cell survival. Although the goal of ADCs in
 > patients is to kill cancer cells, in our experiments we need to
 > identify the concentration at which the drug begins to have an
 > effect, but the cells still survive until the readout.

> Flow cytometry will be the main readout for the titrations:

- We will focus on the two anti-TROP2 ADCs.

- Isotype controls (IC) of both drugs will be included

- A very simple panel will be used: (Viability dye, EpCAM, CD45, FAP)

- We will prioritize the number of conditions to test based on the
 > number of recovered cells on a per-sample basis):

> Prioritization (3 or 4 data points):
>
> 1 drug (ADC1) --- IC
>
> 1 drug (ADC1) --- IC + ADC1
>
> 2 drugs (ADC1 + ADC2) --- IC1 + ADC1 + IC2
>
> 2 drugs (ADC1 + ADC2) --- IC1 + ADC1 + IC2 + ADC2

#### **Action items**

- Kursat will order the isotypes for the corresponding ADCs.

rugs (ADC1 + ADC2) --- IC1 + ADC1 + IC2
>
> 2 drugs (ADC1 + ADC2) --- IC1 + ADC1 + IC2 + ADC2

#### **Action items**

- Kursat will order the isotypes for the corresponding ADCs.

- Kursat will refine and organize the calculations for the titration
 > experiments and double check with María and Sarah once this is
 > ready.

- Kursat will double check the payloads of each of our candidate ADCs
 > (both anti-TROP2, and the backup anti-FRa).

- María will help Sarah familiarize with the sample processing
 > protocol.

- María, Kursat, and Sarah will perform the titration experiments in
 > two patients

- Follow up meeting on 21st of October (11:00 - 12:00)

## 12.08.2025

**Attendees:** María, Aino, NovoGene sales rep and technical specialists

### **Agenda**

- **Greetings and introductions** (5 min)

- **Overview of pilot experiment** (5 min)

- **Discussion: Pricing, technical requirements, etc** (35 min)

### **Notes**

Novogene account can be opened after getting a first quote

Intro to Novogene next-gen sequencing:

Van (sales rep) lives in Sweden, so she takes care of Finland for now,
but there will be a new one soon exclusively for Finland.

Originally the samples were shipped to UK, but after Brexit they moved
the sequencing lab to Munich: IZB Munich Germany

Shipment from Finland should take 1 or 2 days at most, so sample

ally the samples were shipped to UK, but after Brexit they moved
the sequencing lab to Munich: IZB Munich Germany

Shipment from Finland should take 1 or 2 days at most, so sample
degradation upon transport should not be an issue. For this, samples
should be shipped in dry ice.

Novogene counts with Illumina, PacBio, and Nanopore sequencing
technologies

They have fast turn-around time: 3-4 weeks usually, longest one can be 8
weeks. For this our pilot test, results should not take more than 1
month.

> 1\. Project information
>
> 2\. Quotation
>
> 3\. Project initiation
>
> 4\. Sample delivery
>
> 5\. Sample QC
>
> 6\. Sequencing
>
> 7\. Settlement
>
> 8\. Data delivery

After agreeing internally and with the technical about the right
sequencing parameters for our purposes, the next step will be to get a
quote. However, they will only invoice for the samples that will be
actually sequenced. So the quotation doesn't have to match the final
invoice.

They open the samples under CCTV cameras, so the process is recorded in
case of claims.

They can send the data via the internet, but if it is more than 1 TB,
they will send a hard drive that we can keep.

## 05.05.2025

**Attendees:** María, Anniina, Bassel Alsaed, and Narges Moradi

### **Agenda**

- **Greetings and intro round (5-10 min)**

- **Intro to our project by María and Anniina (5 min)**

- **Experimental plan for multiplexed scRNA-seq (40 min)**

**Topics for discussion:**

- What are the costs?

- What are the steps in which we need to start putting money? Is it
 > possible to start by doing mock runs of the things that come
 > before the sequencing and doesn't cost money for me to get
 > familiar with them, and optimize whatever needed?

- Should we do a pilot to start? (e.g. with and without fibroblasts, 3
 > patients)

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Minimum cell requirement

- Minimum viability requirement (do you recommend doing a dead cell

start? (e.g. with and without fibroblasts, 3
 > patients)

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Minimum cell requirement

- Minimum viability requirement (do you recommend doing a dead cell
 > removal step if the viability is usually around 40-50%?)

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Can samples be processed in batches, or is it better to process them
 > individually? Can we for instance combine PATIENTS in the library
 > prep step. SNP array separation of the patients. This might be the
 > most cost efficient but we are not sure if this is possible.

- Suggestions for UMI-tagged antibodies?

- How many conditions can we multiplex per patient?

- Logistics / location / other considerations...???

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- **Wrap up and follow up meeting scheduling (5 min)**

### **Notes**

**Experimental Plan for Multiplexed scRNA-seq (Main Discussion, \~40
min)**

**Technical Considerations & Requirements:**

- **There is a new scRNA-seq method** that allows loading **up to
 > 40,000 cells if needed**; standard is 10,000.

- Goal: load **30,000 cells**, to sequence **20,000**.

- Start with **50,000--100,000 cells/condition**.

- Ideal input for the droplet generation: **150,000 cells total**, to
 > account for possible losses and errors during the prep.

- **Cell concentration**: \~1700--2000 live cells/µL.

**Multiplexing Strategy**

neration: **150,000 cells total**, to
 > account for possible losses and errors during the prep.

- **Cell concentration**: \~1700--2000 live cells/µL.

**Multiplexing Strategy**

**Hashtag C TotalSeq antibodies** support 5\' end sequencing (TCR/BCR
compatible).

- TotalSeq A, B, and C available; C fits 5' end sequencing.

- Antibodies \~450 EUR/antibody; use **2 µL per 10⁶ cells/sample**.

- See: [[BioLegend
 > TotalSeq]{.underline}](https://www.biolegend.com/nl-nl/totalseq/single-cell-rna/multiplex-samples-and-hashtags)

- **Including TCR sequencing does not increase the costs dramatically,
 > might be a very nice option to consider for an extra layer of
 > information.**

- **Number of multiplexed conditions**: 3--5 per sample preferred
 > (Satu multiplexes up to 10 but with cell lines).

- **Avoid using UMI barcode #11** due to known issues.

- **Dead cell removal is recommended**, especially if viability is
 > \~40--50%.

- However, they\'ve successfully run 20--40% viability samples.

- **Fixation of hashed cells**: not tested/validated in their setup.

- It might be possible to pool patients when multiplexing different
 > treatment conditions using either 1) SNPs for patient
 > demultiplexing or 2) UMIs (since the number of treatments will not
 > exceed the number of available UMI-labelled antibodies combos).

nditions using either 1) SNPs for patient
 > demultiplexing or 2) UMIs (since the number of treatments will not
 > exceed the number of available UMI-labelled antibodies combos).

- **Alternative multiplexing** method available: **chip-/bead-based**,
 > less time-consuming than hashing. However, hashing might be the
 > best for us, especially if we can multiplex patients with the
 > UMI-labelled antibodies as well.

**Sample processing strategy**

- Cell counts should be done before and after hashing and preparation.

- Viability check is critical before proceeding.

**Cost Estimates & Logistics**

- Pilot study is highly encouraged (e.g. 3 patients ± fibroblasts).
 > Ideally, also include the drugs that will be tested to get a sense
 > of the impact in viability.

- 10x Chromium Kit: \~500 EUR

- Library prep reagents: price to be clarified (Basel will ask their
 > lab tech for a list of reagents and costs).

- Sequencing costs depend on the provider. Best options are:

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Novogene (Germany) or collaborator in Sweden (They use Novogene)

- 4x cheaper than FIMM.

- Estimated cost: \~2000 EUR/sample, but this depends on
 > depth/project.

- The downside of using Novogene is that the output will be the raw
 > data, without any pre-processing. However, we might have the
 > personnel to deal with this efficiently (eg. Collab with Anna

using Novogene is that the output will be the raw
 > data, without any pre-processing. However, we might have the
 > personnel to deal with this efficiently (eg. Collab with Anna
 > Vähärautio and Wenqing)

**Additional Suggestions**

- If adding TCR sequencing, consider using for instance **benign
 > tissue or peripheral blood** as control.

- **De-multiplexing was the main computational bottleneck** (Johannes,
 > Heidi's bioinformatician worked on this).

- Basel will **perform hashing next week Friday**; a good chance to
 > observe.

- Eliisa Kekäläinen's team may be using a **lower input cell**
 > approach --- worth exploring if needed.

#### **Action items**

- Follow up with Bassel to:

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Gather reagent and price list from their lab tech.

- Coordinate to observe the next hashing on Friday 16.05.2025

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Discuss patient pooling feasibility with Andrew Erickson and/or Anna
 > Vähärautio**.**

- Plan pilot: start with 2-3 patients, +/- fibroblasts, +/- drug of
 > choice.

- **Set a follow up meeting once this is sorted.**
